comments:
Summary and contributions:
The manuscript studies non-Bayesian consensus-based learning in multi-agent networks when an unknown subset of agents may be Byzantine. Two algorithms are proposed: (1) BFL, which combines iterative (vector) Byzantine consensus (Byz-Iter/Tverberg-based) on log-beliefs with local cumulative-likelihood updates, and (2) a modified scheme that reduces per-iteration complexity by decomposing the m-ary problem into pairwise scalar log-likelihood-ratio consensus problems and using scalar Byzantine consensus primitives. Under well-specified network identifiability conditions the authors prove almost-sure convergence of correct agents’ beliefs to the true hypothesis, establish a finite-time high-probability exponential-type decay for the failure-free case, and identify a minimal identifiability condition (for scalar consensus) that is essentially tight.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses an important problem at the intersection of distributed learning and Byzantine fault tolerance. Accounting for adversarial agents in non-Bayesian social learning/consensus frameworks is timely.
- The combination of Byzantine (vector and scalar) consensus primitives with cumulative-likelihood updates is technically natural and well-motivated.
- Theoretical results are fairly comprehensive: almost-sure convergence proofs under clear assumptions, a finite-time high-probability bound for the failure-free case, and a complexity/identifiability trade-off leading to a modified algorithm with weaker conditions independent of m.
- The paper carefully relates its assumptions and proofs to prior work on iterative Byzantine consensus and consensus-based learning, and identifies minimality of the scalar identifiability condition.

Weaknesses and suggestions:
- Computational complexity: the first (vector) scheme relies on Byz-Iter with Tverberg-point computations; the authors acknowledge the high cost and present a modified algorithm with complexity O(m^2 n log n) per agent per iteration. It would improve the paper to provide a more explicit practical comparison of complexity (constants, expected runtime) and to discuss the regimes where the vector approach might still be useful (e.g., small m, small f).
- Assumptions: the convergence guarantees rest on several strong assumptions: synchronous operation, static network topology, knowledge of an upper bound f, and Assumption 2 (identifiability on source components) for the vector case. While some of these are standard, the paper should more clearly discuss necessity vs sufficiency of those assumptions (the paper does so to an extent, but a more explicit exposition and intuition for practitioners would help). In particular, the requirement that each reduced graph contains a unique source component can be restrictive on sparse/directional topologies; more discussion or examples illustrating applicability would be useful.
- Finite-time analysis is provided only for f = 0. The authors note difficulties extending it to f > 0; nevertheless, practitioners would benefit from at least empirical convergence curves (simulations) showing how the algorithms behave in presence of faults and how rates compare to the failure-free bounds.
- No experiments or numerical examples are included in the provided text. Even simple simulations validating the theorems, illustrating the quadratic-in-time exponent behavior in the failure-free case, and comparing the two algorithms (vector vs pairwise) would strengthen the manuscript and clarify practical performance.
- Clarity/formatting: the proofs are dense and rely on several prior results; a short roadmap of the proof techniques and intuition (perhaps a figure illustrating reduced graphs, source components, and information propagation under adversarial trimming) would help the reader. Also the provided text dump contained duplication and some formatting artifacts — presumably a rendering issue — but the manuscript should be carefully proofread.

Other technical comments/questions:
- The algorithms assume agents know the bound f. Discuss sensitivity to an over- or under-estimate of f in practice.
- The modified pairwise scheme requires maintaining O(m^2) scalar processes per agent; discuss tradeoffs and scenarios where m is large (scalability) and potential mitigations (e.g., hierarchical grouping of hypotheses).
- The authors mention generalizability to time-varying and asynchronous networks; a short section sketching which parts of the analysis extend and what new technical obstacles appear would be valuable.

Overall assessment and recommendation:
This is a solid theoretical contribution that advances understanding of Byzantine-robust, consensus-based non-Bayesian learning. The results are meaningful to the distributed learning and fault-tolerant consensus communities. The main limitations are practical (computational cost of the vector scheme) and the lack of empirical validation in the provided text. I recommend publication after addressing the suggestions above (add simulations/illustrative examples, expand discussion of assumptions, and clarify complexity trade-offs).
